So, on entry to `localizeKey`, the` privKey` is not the complete private key yet, it is missing the `P2K(K1,<engine_id>)` step. So the call to:
  `localPrivateKey = localizeKeyMD5/SHA(privKey, snmpEngineId)`
completes the private key, and this is stored in `localPrivateKey`.  

At this point, the key is too short, so then enter the loop where both steps of `hashPassphraseMD5/SHA` and `localizeKeyMD5/SHA` are done until the key is long enough (this is as per the draft standard as you noted).

Then the private key of the right length is returned from the `localPrivKey` variable.

> or you could do combinations of passed in --platform and --abi args like you mentioned. Either seems reasonable to me.

I think the problem with that is that there are no natural combinations here at the ABI level in one of the most common native cases we have/see: a OSX + Linux multi-platform resolve (aka how we build almost every pex at Twitter). OSX interpreters are traditionally always `m` while Linux is traditionally always `mu`. so in that common case, the combinatorial approach would then end up with two completely extraneous platforms (osx->mu, linux->m) which would need to be 1) pre-built/available and 2) resolved - for no apparent benefit. I also think relying on users to pass in position-dependent sets of all 4 facets is likely to be error prone.

so fundamentally to me, it seems like any form of split args for all 4 facets here - which are now necessary to pass all or none - will lead to an awkward, complicated interface for the CLI. having one clear way to pass in the platform + tags triple in all cases would be IMHO cleanest/most ideal - vs having one set of args you pass in for single-platform resolves (e.g. `--platform X --python-version X --implementation X --abi X`) and then an entirely different passing style when you want to pass in a second platform (e.g. `--tags X --tags X`). so I think that means either outright replacing or extending `--platform`.

fwiw, my implicit thinking with the idea of extending `--platform` is largely around backward compatibility. I had alluded briefly to the idea of implicit `any` wildcards for the latter 3 fields, which I think could be a good mechanism for that.

the idea there essentially is that:

```
./pants --platform=linux-x86_64 ...
```

could implicitly map to e.g.:

```
./pants --platform=linux-x86_64-any-any-any ...
```

so that existing users could continue to pass in `--platform` as they do today and get the ~current behavior. then, when the extended form is needed that could be passed in as well just as easily. I was thinking about tackling the backwards compatibility aspect as a fast-follow on top of your work here, prior to cutting a release.

it's also worth noting that this idea of overloading platform and remaining backward compatible would also readily extend to pants as well, where platforms are specified as an iterable of platform strings that could also work with either form to ease the need for migration on users (..tho pants is not exactly affected by the CLI API discussed here, the conceptual mapping and underlying functionality in pex to support is relevant). additionally, `tags` also has an existing conceptual meaning in pants that applies to all targets.

but I definitely hear you re: murky terminology - and this is certainly overloading the term `platform` (and `tags` too, unfortunately).. so I'm open to alternative tacks.

either way tho, it'd probably be a good idea to get some other perspectives on this as well. maybe @jsirois might be able to review and share his take?

This could probably be converted into:
```python
for cur_page in pages:
  html_filename = '{}.html'.format(cur_page)
  page_is_here = cur_page == here
  if page_is_here:
    link = html_filename
  else:
    link = os.path.relpath(html_filename, os.path.dirname(here))
  links.append(dict(
    link=link,
    text=precomputed.page[cur_page].title,
    here=page_is_here))
```

The only thing I would say should almost definitely change is just using `.format()`, which is generally preferred to string concatenation in almost all cases. The rest just seemed to make it easier to follow, especially using `'{}.html'.format(cur_page)` instead of using `here` in the first branch of the `if` -- since `i == here`, then `i + '.html'` is the same as `here + '.html'`, so it's easier to make that into a single statement. Let me know if I'm getting something wrong -- this kind of edit isn't something you need to add but I would recommend if it makes sense.

Hehe. The `--` is actually necessary, as it tells `runuser` to stop processing arguments.

No, the real issue here is that `.split()` produces the following list:

```python
['runuser', '-u', 'alice', '--', 'ssh-keygen', '-N', '-f', '/home/alice/.ssh/mykey']
```

Plumbum then passes `''` as the *literal* argument to `-N`, thus instructing `ssh-keygen` to make a key with the passphrase `''`.

The above translates to the following bash command:

```bash
runuser -u alice -- ssh-keygen -N "''" -f /home/alice/.ssh/mykey
```

In your cited example, if the tags aren't in dest, the manifests won't be either. We will know there is a problem and we will fix it. I don't think that's a benefit.

Also, the general pattern in pulp-smash of performing all the "work" in setUpClass means that we need a lot more classes. This means that we sync from docker n times instead of one time in this copy module. I'm not a fan of that, and I'm sure Docker wouldn't be either. If anything, we should have one setUpClass that does the sync, and then each test should do whatever copying it needs along with whatever assertions it needs.

It's easy to add assertions to tests, so I don't think that benefit is meaningfully different between the two approaches.
